text.title: niche-evaluation Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for niche-evaluation. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Define the niche precisely using this formula: [Product Type] for [Specific Audience] who [Specific Problem].
rule.rationale: Vague definitions prevent effective market research and evaluation. "Project management software" is unsearchable; "task tracking for freelance designers who struggle with client deadlines" is specific enough to research and distinguish from competitors.

rule.statement: Calculate potential using TAM (Total Addressable Market), SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market), and SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market). Targets: TAM ≥$1B, SAM ≥$100M, SOM ≥$1M.
rule.rationale: These metrics provide a reality-check on market scale. A niche with TAM <$50M is unlikely to sustain a venture; one with SOM <$1M won't even cover living expenses for a solopreneur.

rule.statement: Quick validation using Google Trends (is interest growing?), keyword volume (are people searching?), and community size (are people talking?).
rule.rationale: Declining interest curves and low search volume signal a shrinking niche; community size (Reddit/Discord) indicates demand density and researchability.

rule.statement: Map competitors on a 2x2 matrix: Quality vs. Number of Players. Target position: High quality, few competitors.
rule.rationale: Quadrants reveal strategy: Opportunity Zone (high quality, low competition) is ideal; Premium segment (high quality, many) means proven market but crowded; Race to Bottom (low quality, many) requires speed; Crowded Market (low quality, high quantity) is red flag.

rule.statement: Score audience accessibility 1-5 across: organic reach (Reddit/Twitter/etc), dedicated communities (Discord/Slack), media presence (podcasts/blogs), paid ads availability, and spending power.
rule.rationale: Target average score >3.5. Low accessibility (score <2) means high customer acquisition cost and makes validation interviews hard. If you can't reach the audience affordably, the niche is inefficient regardless of size.

rule.statement: Evaluate revenue models (SaaS, course, templates, consulting, ads) and price points. Question: Can you charge $50+/month or $200+ one-time?
rule.rationale: Willingness to pay is the binding constraint. A large niche that pays $5/month cannot sustain a solopreneur; a small niche paying $500/mo is more viable. Avoid niches where the cost-to-serve > revenue.

rule.statement: Rate honestly on a 1-5 scale: audience understanding, relevant skills, daily excitement, content-creation ability, network connections. If average <3, reconsider even if market is great.
rule.rationale: Passion matters but won't compensate for bad market. Conversely, a good market with low personal fit means you'll burn out or produce mediocre work. This dimension must come from a self-interview, not auto-scored by agents.

rule.statement: Compute final score: (Market × 0.25) + (Competition × 0.20) + (Accessibility × 0.15) + (Monetization × 0.20) + (Personal Fit × 0.20). Thresholds: >4.0 Excellent, 3.5-4.0 Good, 3.0-3.5 Risky, <3.0 Reconsider.
rule.rationale: The weights reflect priority: market size dominates, personal fit and monetization are co-equal gates, competition and accessibility are supporting factors. These proportions are heuristic, not calibrated; adjust for your risk tolerance.
